drills.scenario: A house-flipping coach wants to scale a cold campaign to a large, broad audience on Meta. His current ad opens with the line 'How to flip houses' and reach has plateaued. He keeps raising budget but CPMs climb and results dry up.
drills.question: Why is this hook capping his scale, and what should he do?
options: The hook is too curiosity-driven — make it more literal so more people understand it

A generic solution-name hook like 'how to flip houses' only resonates with the small Solution-Aware segment, creating a scalability problem; switch to a more specific or curiosity-driven hook to reach less-aware audiences
The hook is fine — the problem is purely budget; keep raising spend until the algorithm catches up
Add his credentials to the first line so cold audiences trust him faster
drills.explanation: UPSYD / Awareness Staircase: a generic solution-name hook ('how to flip houses') lands only on Solution-Aware prospects, a small segment — so it cannot scale. To reach the larger, less-aware market you must lead with a more specific or curiosity-driven hook and educate first. Leading with the solution name to broad audiences is an explicit pitfall.

drills.scenario: A Shopify supplement brand sells a greens powder. Their cold-traffic ad's first line of post copy reads: 'Looking for an easy way to get more superfoods?' The strategist now needs to write the headline and is debating whether to repeat the theme or introduce a brand-new angle.
drills.question: Per the 13 Elements 'Ask Questions' pattern, what is the best headline?
options: State a discount: 'Save 20% on our greens powder today only'
Introduce a fresh, unrelated angle: 'The #1 mistake people make with vitamins'
Restate the same theme as a benefit statement: 'Get all your healthy superfoods in one drink'
List the credentials: 'Formulated by a board-certified nutritionist'
drills.explanation: Element 5 (Ask Questions): open the first line of post copy with a question that addresses your hook, then restate that SAME question as a benefit statement in the headline — same theme, two ways, two places. 'Looking for an easy way to get more superfoods?' pairs with 'Get all your healthy superfoods in one drink.' Leading with a discount or credentials is a separate pitfall.

drills.scenario: A keto nutrition coach is crafting a new contrarian hook for a broad, mostly Problem-Aware cold audience. She wants to engineer it the way the 'Is breakfast really the most important meal of the day?' hook was built, then validate it before writing the full ad.
drills.question: Put the hook-building steps in the correct order, from first to last.
options: Identify the avatar's real pain point the hook must speak to (e.g., never has time to eat breakfast, feels guilty about it)
Pick a conventional myth held by millions to challenge, and frame it as a curiosity-based, contrarian question that opens a knowledge gap
Make sure the hook also educates — promises something new and interesting, not just a provocation
Google the hook (search the question and problem phrases, scan FAQs and the suggestion tool) to confirm market interest and find contrarian angles before writing the ad
drills.explanation: This mirrors the Ketogenic Living 4-criteria checklist plus the 'Google the hook' research method: (1) anchor to the avatar's pain, (2) pick a widely-held myth and frame a curiosity-based contrarian question (Knowledge Gap), (3) ensure it educates/informs on something new, then (4) validate demand and harvest angles by Googling the hook before writing the full ad.

drills.scenario: A B2B SaaS founder is writing a Meta lead-gen ad to a cold, broad audience. He drafts the opening line: 'Download your FREE Facebook Lead-Tracking Cheat Sheet now!' Reach is fine but link CTR is dismal and the few clicks don't convert.
drills.question: Diagnose the problem with this opening line.
options: The lead magnet is free, and free offers always underperform paid ones
The line leads with the solution name and the offer (the 'bait'), but a broad/less-aware audience needs the hook — a curiosity or pain message — before they care about the offer
The word 'now' creates false urgency that Meta penalizes
The line is too long for a first line of post copy
drills.explanation: Hook is not equal to Offer: the offer is the action (download the cheat sheet); the hook is how you sell it. Leading with the solution name / offer to a broad, less-aware audience is the documented pitfall ('Download your FREE ... cheat sheet now!' only lands on Your-Solution-Aware people). For broad traffic, open with a curiosity or pain hook and present the offer after intriguing them.

drills.scenario: A nonprofit is shooting a 30-second Meta video ad to recruit volunteers. The creative director wants the first 3-10 seconds to reliably stop the scroll for both sound-on and sound-off viewers.
drills.question: Select ALL tactics the module recommends for the 'Stop the Scroll' opening.
options: Blatant hand motions — wave at the viewer or pull them toward you like a store demonstrator
Open on a slow, scenic logo animation to establish brand polish first
Add text overlay / captions so sound-off viewers get the message (and we read faster than we speak)
Use a pattern interrupt — an unusual shot, extreme close-up, or selfie-style look
drills.explanation: Three-Step Video Formula, Step 1 (Stop the Scroll), first 3-10 seconds: (1) blatant hand motions, (2) text overlay/captions for sound-off viewers and faster delivery, (3) a pattern interrupt that makes the picture look unusual. A slow scenic logo intro does the opposite — it lets the thumb keep swiping before you ever hook them.

drills.scenario: A productivity-app founder wants a single ad that will travel — getting organic shares so Meta rewards it with cheaper impressions. He has a genuinely counterintuitive, useful insight: most to-do lists actually make you LESS productive. He's deciding how to frame the hook to maximize sharing.
drills.question: Which framing best leverages the module's sharing principles?
options: A polished product feature tour: 'See every feature of our new app'
An 'Aha Moment' hook that exposes a common belief as a myth and teaches the surprising insight, giving people practical value and social currency to share
A heavy discount banner: 'Lifetime access, 70% off this week'
A celebrity name-drop with no insight attached
drills.explanation: Building Block #5 'Aha Moment' (expose a common belief as a myth + valuable content) is HUGE for the algorithm — more shares mean cheaper clicks/impressions and higher ROI. It also stacks STEPPS principles: Social Currency (sharing makes them look smart) and Practical Value (something they can use). A feature tour or discount carries no shareable insight.
